title: Agentic Healthcare Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for clinical decision-support agents — differentials, dosing, guideline lookup, chart summarization — where the action behind the gate is the highest-stakes in the series: harm to a person. It sits above the Legal vertical on the irreversibility gradient. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds the things that make healthcare distinct: grounding in clinical guidelines and the patient record under PHI scope, a confidence calibrator and a deterministic safety layer the model cannot override, and a gate whose safe default is to abstain — decline to answer — rather than guess, with a licensed clinician authorizing every recommendation. Nothing acts on a patient autonomously.

nodes.label: Clinician
nodes.description: Licensed clinician who reviews and authorizes every recommendation; the decision-maker.

nodes.label: Patient
nodes.description: The person the care concerns; their PHI must stay scoped to their care team.

nodes.label: Clinical Query
nodes.description: A decision-support request: differential, dosing, guideline lookup, summary.

nodes.label: Clinical API
nodes.description: Authenticates the request, resolves care-team scope, routes to the pipeline.

nodes.label: Decision-Support Pipeline
nodes.description: Deterministic DAG: retrieve -> reason -> calibrate -> abstain-or-recommend -> clinician sign-off.

nodes.label: Reasoning Agent
nodes.description: LLM in a bounded node: drafts a grounded recommendation with an explicit confidence and citations.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform), under BAA / no-train terms.

nodes.label: Clinical Guideline Index
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: evidence-based guidelines, formularies, drug-interaction data.

nodes.label: EHR / Patient Record
nodes.description: The patient's chart: meds, labs, problems, allergies. The action and grounding surface.

nodes.label: Confidence Calibrator
nodes.description: Deterministic check of grounding sufficiency and calibrated confidence; triggers abstention.

nodes.label: Deterministic Safety Layer
nodes.description: Hard rules the model cannot override: contraindication/interaction/allergy checks, dose limits.

nodes.label: Sign-off & Abstain Gate
nodes.description: Routes to clinician sign-off; abstains (declines to answer) when grounding or confidence is insufficient.

nodes.label: Recommendation Store
nodes.description: Source of truth for each recommendation_case and its state machine (drafted, blocked_by_safety, abstained, awaiting_signoff, modified, authorized, rejected, expired, ehr_write_failed).

nodes.label: Clinician Worklist / Review UI
nodes.description: The review surface backed by RecStore: pending sign-offs, abstentions to review, and safety-blocked cases, triaged by urgency and risk tier; where the clinician authorizes, modifies, rejects, or asks for more evidence.

nodes.label: PHI-Scoped Retrieval
nodes.description: Identity-scoped retrieval: only the patient's own record, for their care team.

nodes.label: Privacy & Policy
nodes.description: HIPAA/PHI scoping, tenant isolation, and screening of untrusted note/record text.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access; binds the decision to a licensed clinician principal.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: sources + versions, confidence, safety checks, abstentions, clinician decisions.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, calibration and abstention metrics, harm-avoidance eval vs clinician ground truth.

functional: Answer clinical decision-support queries (differential, dosing, guideline lookup, chart summary).
Ground every recommendation in evidence-based guidelines and the patient's record.
Run deterministic safety checks (contraindications, interactions, allergies, dose limits).
Abstain — decline to answer — when grounding or confidence is insufficient.
Require a licensed clinician to review and authorize every recommendation before it acts.
Scope behavior by clinical task and risk tier: dosing, differential, chart summary, and guideline lookup don't share one confidence threshold or one approval path.
nonFunctional: The action behind the gate is harm to a person: safety dominates; the safe default is to abstain, not guess.
PHI privacy: identity-scoped retrieval (only the patient's own record, for their care team); HIPAA / BAA; no-train.
A deterministic safety layer enforces hard rules the model cannot override.
A documented safety case; SaMD/FDA framing for any diagnostic claim.
Auditable: sources, confidence, safety checks, abstentions, and clinician decisions recorded.

capacity.label: Active clinicians
capacity.note: Large health system; clinicians on shift concentrate load into daytime hours.

capacity.label: Decision-support queries
capacity.note: ~25 queries per active clinician per day across dosing, differentials, lookups, and summaries.

capacity.label: Peak interactive QPS
capacity.note: Diurnal peak (rounds, shift change) is ~5-8x the daily average; size for the peak, not the mean.

capacity.label: EHR retrieval fanout
capacity.note: Meds, labs, problems, allergies, encounters; FHIR/HL7 reads dominate per-query latency and hit EHR rate limits.

capacity.label: Guideline index latency
capacity.note: Vector + keyword retrieval over versioned guidelines/formularies; cached hot.

capacity.label: Interactive p95 latency
capacity.note: Retrieve -> reason -> calibrate -> safety for a synchronous query; longer tasks go async with a worklist entry.

capacity.label: Inference budget
capacity.note: Bounded context (chart facts + cited guideline spans); concurrency capped per tenant by the shared serving tier.

capacity.label: Sign-off queue depth
capacity.note: Worklist age and depth are first-class: an over-eager system that floods the queue is an operational failure (alert fatigue).

capacity.label: EHR write rate limit
capacity.note: Signed-off writes are throttled to EHR limits with idempotent retry; a failed write becomes an explicit ehr_write_failed state.

capacity.label: Backpressure
capacity.note: Async tasks are queued and load-leveled; under overload, admission control sheds non-urgent work and prioritizes urgent cases rather than degrading safety.

capacity.label: Default on uncertainty
capacity.note: When grounding or calibrated confidence is below the task/risk-tier threshold, the platform declines rather than answers.

capacity.label: Autonomy
capacity.note: A clinician authorizes every recommendation; nothing orders or acts on a patient autonomously.

capacity.label: Safety override
capacity.note: Hard contraindication/interaction/dose rules can block a recommendation the model would otherwise make.

api.description: The pipeline produces a grounded recommendation with a calibrated confidence, or an abstention, for clinician review — it never acts on the patient. The request carries the clinician principal, tenant/org, encounter and care-team scope, the clinical task type and risk tier (which select the threshold and approval path), urgency, and an idempotency key so a retried submission returns the same case rather than a duplicate.

A clinician's review worklist: cases awaiting sign-off, abstentions needing review, and safety-blocked cases, ordered by urgency and risk tier. This is the surface that makes the human-in-the-loop concrete and exposes alert-fatigue load (queue depth, age).
api.request: (clinician principal from token; filters: status, urgency, taskType)

api.description: A licensed clinician authorizes, modifies, rejects, or requests more evidence. Authorization names the exact EHR action being signed for and is idempotent on the case version; the response reports whether the downstream EHR write succeeded so a write failure is an explicit, retryable state rather than a silent loss.

Fetch the full safety case for the recommendation: evidence bundle (guideline/formulary versions, citation spans, patient-fact snapshot), model/prompt/tool versions, calibration features and result, safety-rule version and per-check results, abstention reason, clinician decision metadata, and the EHR write outcome — enough to reconstruct exactly why the system did what it did.

dataModel.name: recommendation_case
dataModel.note: First-class case: one clinical query and everything needed to reconstruct its safety case. Child records hang off case_id.

dataModel.name: evidence_bundle
dataModel.note: The exact grounding used, so a recommendation is reproducible.

dataModel.name: calibration_result
dataModel.note: Why the case passed or routed to abstention.

dataModel.name: safety_checks
dataModel.note: Deterministic safety results for a case, with versioned rules and fail-closed warn state.

dataModel.name: signoff_decision
dataModel.note: Clinician decision metadata and the exact authorized action.

dataModel.name: audit_record
dataModel.note: Append-only audit. PHI-access events are recorded even when no recommendation is authorized, separately from recommendation events.

selfHosted: FHIR / HL7v2 integration engine (Mirth Connect / NextGen Connect)
SMART on FHIR app + bulk FHIR export
tradeoff: Managed FHIR stores remove a lot of HL7/FHIR plumbing but you still own per-EHR quirks, rate limits, and write semantics.
Reads and writes go through real EHR rate limits — budget retry/idempotency around every chart write.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed FHIR service removes the need to build and operate your own HL7v2 parser and FHIR persistence layer.

tradeoff: Hybrid (keyword + vector) retrieval beats pure-vector for clinical lookups where exact drug/condition terms matter.
Whatever you pick, version the corpus so a recommendation cites the guideline/formulary version it actually used.

selfHosted: vLLM / TGI on owned GPUs (full data control)
tradeoff: Managed inference is faster to ship but only acceptable under a signed BAA with no-train / no-retention terms.
Self-hosting gives maximal data control at the cost of running a GPU serving tier.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A BAA-covered managed endpoint removes the need to operate your own GPU fleet just to keep PHI in-boundary.

selfHosted: Drug-interaction / formulary dataset (First Databank, RxNorm, Multum)
Rules engine (Drools / OPA) with versioned rule sets
tradeoff: Clinical rule content is typically licensed; keep the engine and rule versions yours and auditable.
Rules must be versioned per case so a past decision can be reproduced after the rules change.

selfHosted: Append-only Postgres / Kafka with WORM retention
tradeoff: PHI-access audit and recommendation audit have different retention and access rules — separate them.
Immutability/WORM is what makes the safety case defensible, not just queryable.

selfHosted: Offline eval harness with clinician-labeled ground truth
tradeoff: Track confident-wrong rate, abstention precision/recall, and calibration per task/specialty, not just top-line accuracy.
An LLM-as-judge can triage trajectories but is auxiliary; clinician adjudication is the source of truth.

patterns.name: Agent harness (from Foundations)
patterns.what: The deterministic runtime that wraps the model and turns one completion into an agent: it owns the loop, assembles context each turn, parses and executes tool calls, feeds observations back, and enforces stop conditions, retries, compaction, and budgets. This platform consumes the harness from Agentic Platform Foundations rather than reimplementing it.
patterns.whenToUse: The clinical decision-support pipeline runs on this harness, with the deterministic safety layer and human-clinician abstain gate around the Decision-Support Pipeline layered on top of it.

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (retrieve -> reason -> calibrate -> abstain-or-recommend), not an autonomous loop.
patterns.whenToUse: Where a wrong action can harm a patient.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A licensed clinician authorizes every recommendation before it acts.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent reasons freely but a calibrator, safety layer, and clinician sign-off fence the action.
patterns.whenToUse: Recommend-only autonomy with hard safety boundaries.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground in clinical guidelines and the patient record, not model memory.
patterns.whenToUse: Every recommendation must cite evidence and patient data.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate calibration, abstention quality, and harm-avoidance against clinician ground truth.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: PHI-scoped access; the decision is bound to a licensed clinician principal.
patterns.whenToUse: Accessing the chart and recording authorized actions.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat free-text notes and records as untrusted; partition from EHR write authority.
patterns.whenToUse: Notes and records are attacker-reachable input.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: Queue async decision-support work and the sign-off worklist; under overload, admission control sheds non-urgent work and prioritizes urgent cases rather than degrading safety or flooding clinicians.
patterns.whenToUse: Diurnal clinical query peaks and a bounded clinician review capacity (worklist depth and case age are first-class).

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Answers Clinical Questions (the baseline)
description: Start with the tempting demo: a clinician (or a patient) asks a clinical question and the agent answers directly from the model — a dose, a differential, an interpretation.

In healthcare this is the most dangerous baseline in the whole series. A confident wrong answer can harm a patient; the model has no grounding in this patient's chart or current guidelines, no safety checks for allergies or interactions, no notion of when it should refuse, and no clinician in the loop. Unlike a bad PR or even a bad filing, the side effect can be irreversible bodily harm.
The rest of the interview adds grounding, calibration, a deterministic safety layer, and a clinician sign-off whose default is to abstain.

The Decision-Support Pipeline runs on the agent harness from Agentic Platform Foundations — the runtime that owns the loop, assembles context, and mediates every tool call — so this platform adds clinical safety checks and the human-clinician decision gate on top of that harness rather than rebuilding the loop.

view.caption: An agent answers clinical questions straight from the model — no chart grounding, no safety checks, no clinician gate. The highest-stakes baseline in the series.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a confidently wrong clinical answer more dangerous than a wrong legal citation?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive direct answer makes the safety, grounding, and abstention requirements concrete.

concepts.definition: The deterministic runtime that wraps the model and drives the agent loop — context assembly, tool-call parsing/execution, and budget/stop-condition enforcement. Distinct from an eval harness (offline scoring).
concepts.whyItMatters: The Decision-Support Pipeline runs on this harness, and the clinical safety layer and human-clinician abstain gate are layered on top of it rather than rebuilt inside the loop.
concepts.example: A clinical request enters the harness, which assembles the patient context and guidelines, runs the scoped reasoning agent, and routes the recommendation to a human clinician — never auto-acts on the patient.

recap.after: An agent can answer a clinical question.
recap.newRisk: No grounding, no safety checks, no clinician, and no ability to refuse — a wrong answer can harm a patient.

traps.trap: Letting the agent answer clinical questions directly because it sounds authoritative.
traps.why: Fluency is not safety; without grounding, safety checks, and a clinician, a confident error can harm a patient.
traps.instead: Ground in guidelines and the chart, check safety deterministically, abstain when unsure, and require sign-off.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Guidelines + the Patient Record
description: The grounding corpus is two-sided: evidence-based clinical guidelines and formularies, plus this patient's record (meds, labs, problems, allergies). A recommendation must be grounded in both — the right guideline for the right patient.

Retrieval of the chart is PHI-scoped (next step); guidelines, formularies, and drug-interaction data are versioned so the agent reasons from current evidence, not stale or generic knowledge. The exact versions and the patient-fact snapshot used are captured on the recommendation so the safety case is reproducible — and a stale or incomplete snapshot (e.g. a medication list older than the freshness window, a missing allergy list) is itself a reason to abstain or request review rather than recommend.

view.caption: Recommendations are grounded in two sources: versioned clinical guidelines/formularies and the patient's own record (retrieved PHI-scoped through PHIScope).

steps.decisionPrompt: Why must a recommendation be grounded in BOTH the guideline and this patient's chart?

concepts.definition: Grounding a recommendation in both the current guideline and this patient's chart (meds, labs, allergies, problems).
concepts.whyItMatters: A guideline-correct answer can still be wrong for this patient (an allergy, an interaction, a contraindication).
concepts.example: A standard antibiotic is guideline-correct but contraindicated by a documented allergy in the chart.

whyNow: Grounding is the basis for both safety checks and calibrated confidence.

recap.before: The agent answered from generic model knowledge.
recap.after: Recommendations are grounded in guidelines and the patient's chart.
recap.newRisk: Grounding doesn't guarantee the agent knows when its grounding is insufficient.

steps.title: 3. Calibration & Abstention: Knowing When Not to Answer
description: The defining safety mechanism: the agent must know when it doesn't know. 'Confidence' here is not the model's self-reported number — a model self-score alone is not sufficient. The calibrator combines three separable signals: grounding/evidence sufficiency (did retrieval actually find the guideline spans and patient facts the task needs?), a calibrated uncertainty score (the raw model score mapped onto observed correctness, plus contradiction checks across sources and an out-of-distribution flag for unfamiliar presentations), and a risk-tier policy that sets the threshold by task — chart summarization and guideline lookup tolerate a lower bar than dosing or differential diagnosis. If grounding is insufficient or the calibrated score is below the task's threshold, the path leads to abstention rather than a guess.

Thresholds are not static: they are set per task and risk tier, validated against clinician ground truth, and watched for drift (a rising confident-wrong rate or a shifting score distribution is a signal to retune, not to ship). Abstaining — explicitly declining and escalating — is a successful outcome here, not a failure. A wrong confident answer is worse than 'I can't determine this; refer to a clinician'.

view.caption: A calibrator checks grounding sufficiency and confidence; insufficient evidence routes to abstention, not a guess.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is 'I can't determine this' a successful output, and how do you detect when to say it?

options.name: Risk-tiered thresholds + calibrated evaluator (default)
options.description: The calibrator combines grounding sufficiency, a calibrated uncertainty score, and a per-task/per-risk-tier threshold. Dosing and differential diagnosis carry a higher bar than chart summary or guideline lookup.
pros: Matches the abstention bar to the harm of being wrong on that task.
Calibrated score (mapped to observed correctness) is far more trustworthy than a raw model self-score.
Thresholds are auditable and tunable per task as drift is detected.
cons: More to build and validate: per-task ground-truth sets and calibration curves.
Threshold sprawl needs governance so tiers don't drift apart silently.

view.caption: Per-task, risk-tiered thresholds over a calibrated score and grounding-sufficiency check decide pass vs abstain.

options.name: Single global threshold on the model self-score
options.description: One confidence cutoff applied to the model's own reported score across every task.
cons: A model self-score is poorly calibrated and gameable; a high number is not the same as being right.
One threshold over-abstains on low-risk lookups and under-abstains on high-risk dosing — the worst of both.
No per-task signal to detect drift where it matters most.

concepts.definition: The platform declines to answer when grounding or calibrated confidence is below threshold, rather than producing a low-confidence recommendation.
concepts.whyItMatters: In a harm-to-a-person domain, a confident wrong answer is the worst outcome; abstention is the safe default.
concepts.example: Sparse chart data on a rare presentation -> abstain and route to a clinician.

whyNow: Calibration is the safety primitive the gate depends on; it precedes the deterministic safety layer.

recap.before: The agent always produced an answer.
recap.after: The agent abstains when grounding or confidence is insufficient.
recap.newRisk: Even a confident, grounded recommendation can be unsafe for hard rules (allergies, interactions).

steps.title: 4. The Deterministic Safety Layer
description: Some failures must never depend on the model. A deterministic safety layer enforces hard rules the model cannot override: contraindications, drug-drug interactions, documented allergies, and dose limits. It consumes explicit inputs — the patient facts from the chart and the versioned rule and formulary sources it checks against — and returns an explicit per-check result: pass, block, warn, or missing_data. The rule and formulary versions are recorded on the case so a decision can be reproduced even after the rules change.

Crucially it fails closed. A contraindication block and a missing-fact result are different operational outcomes: a block stops the recommendation outright; missing a required fact (no allergy list, a lab too stale to trust) does not pass by default — it routes to abstention or clinician review rather than assuming the chart is complete. If a check blocks, the recommendation is stopped regardless of what the model proposed. This is the fail-safe floor beneath the probabilistic reasoning — the same deterministic-floor idea as Finance and Legal, but the floor protects a body.

view.caption: A deterministic safety layer enforces contraindication, interaction, allergy, and dose rules the model cannot override.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which checks must be deterministic and un-overridable by the model, and why?

options.name: Fail closed on missing/stale facts (default)
options.description: If a required fact for a check is absent or too stale to trust (no allergy list, an out-of-window lab), the layer returns missing_data and routes to abstention or clinician review rather than letting the recommendation pass.
pros: A blank allergy list never reads as 'no allergies'; absence of data is not evidence of safety.
Makes stale-chart risk an explicit, handled outcome rather than a silent assumption.
Distinguishes a contraindication block from a missing-fact escalation — different operational responses.
cons: More abstentions/escalations when charts are incomplete, adding clinician load.
Requires defining freshness windows and required-fact sets per task.

view.caption: Missing or stale required facts return missing_data and fail closed to abstention/review, not a pass.

options.name: Fail open when facts are missing
options.description: If a check can't find the data it needs, treat it as passing and let the recommendation proceed to sign-off.
pros: Fewer abstentions; smoother clinician experience on incomplete charts.
cons: Silently turns 'we don't know' into 'it's safe' — exactly the harm this layer exists to prevent.
A missing allergy record could let a contraindicated drug through to the worklist as if cleared.
Unacceptable in a harm-to-a-person domain; the safe floor must assume the worst on missing data.

concepts.definition: Hard-coded clinical safety rules (contraindications, interactions, allergies, dose limits) evaluated independently of the model.
concepts.whyItMatters: Life-critical checks must not rely on a probabilistic model that can be wrong or fooled.
concepts.example: The model suggests a drug; the safety layer blocks it on a documented allergy.

whyNow: The safety floor must wrap the recommendation before any human even sees it.

recap.before: Safety depended on the model getting it right.
recap.after: Hard rules block unsafe recommendations independently of the model.
recap.newRisk: A grounded, safe recommendation still must not act on a patient without a clinician.

traps.trap: Trusting the model to remember every contraindication.
traps.why: Models are probabilistic and can be injected or simply wrong; life-critical rules can't ride on that.

steps.title: 5. The Gate: Clinician Sign-off (Recommend, Never Act)
description: This is the crux. The platform recommends; a licensed clinician decides. Every recommendation that clears calibration and the safety layer is routed for clinician sign-off, and identity binds the authorized action to a clinician principal — nothing orders, prescribes, or charts on a patient autonomously.

Abstentions are surfaced too: the clinician sees 'the system declined and why'. The agent is decision support, not the decision-maker — which keeps responsibility, and the regulatory line, with the clinician.

The hard part is the workflow, not the single call. Each case lives in a state machine in the Recommendation Store — drafted, blocked_by_safety, abstained, awaiting_signoff, modified_by_clinician, authorized, rejected, expired, ehr_write_failed — surfaced on a Clinician Worklist triaged by urgency and risk tier. A clinician can authorize the exact action, modify it (e.g. adjust a dose), reject with a reason, or request more evidence; an unreviewed case eventually expires rather than lingering; and a signed-off EHR write that fails becomes an explicit ehr_write_failed state to retry, never a silent loss. The worklist's depth and case age are operational signals: flooding it is how a 'safe' system fails through alert fatigue.

view.caption: Cases land in a state machine in the Recommendation Store and surface on the clinician worklist; the clinician authorizes, modifies, rejects, or asks for evidence, and identity binds an authorized action to a clinician before any EHR write.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the difference between decision support and an autonomous clinical action, and where is that line enforced?

options.name: Recommend-only: clinician authorizes every action (default)
options.description: The system writes a pending case to the worklist; an authorized EHR write happens only after a licensed clinician signs off, bound to their principal.
pros: Keeps clinical responsibility and the regulatory/liability line with the clinician.
No order, prescription, or chart entry happens on a patient autonomously.
Modify/reject/request-evidence give the clinician real control, not a rubber stamp.
cons: Throughput is bounded by clinician review capacity.
Only safe if the worklist is well-triaged; flooding it causes alert fatigue and rubber-stamping.

view.caption: The clinician authorizes before any EHR write; the system never acts on the patient on its own.

options.name: Direct EHR write for 'low-risk' actions
options.description: Let the agent write certain low-risk actions to the EHR automatically, reserving sign-off for high-risk ones.
pros: Higher throughput; less clinician review load on routine tasks.
cons: Crosses the bright line from decision support to autonomous clinical action.
'Low-risk' is itself a clinical judgment that can be wrong; an injected note or stale fact can make a 'routine' write harmful.
Moves responsibility and the regulatory line onto the platform — the wrong place for harm-to-a-person.

concepts.definition: The agent recommends; a licensed clinician reviews and authorizes; the system never acts on a patient on its own.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps clinical responsibility and the regulatory/liability line with the clinician.
concepts.example: A dosing recommendation is shown to the clinician, who authorizes, adjusts, or rejects it.

concepts.term: review worklist as a state machine
concepts.definition: Each case moves through explicit states (drafted, blocked_by_safety, abstained, awaiting_signoff, modified_by_clinician, authorized, rejected, expired, ehr_write_failed) on a triaged clinician worklist.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single sign-off call hides the real workflow; modify/reject/request-evidence/expire/retry are where a human-in-the-loop system is actually safe or actually thin.
concepts.example: An unreviewed urgent case ages on the worklist and escalates; a signed-off write that fails the EHR becomes ehr_write_failed to retry, not a silent loss.

traps.trap: Modeling the gate as one sign-off button.
traps.why: Real review needs modify, reject-with-reason, request-more-evidence, expiry, and EHR-write-failure handling; without them the human-in-the-loop is architecturally thin and clinicians rubber-stamp.
traps.instead: Back the gate with a state machine and a triaged worklist, and watch queue depth/age as an alert-fatigue signal.

whyNow: The clinician gate is what makes recommend-only autonomy safe for a harm-to-a-person action.

messages.label: surface, triaged by urgency + risk tier

recap.before: A recommendation could be treated as an action.
recap.after: Only clinician-authorized recommendations act; the agent stays decision support.
recap.newRisk: PHI must be protected and every recommendation defensible under a safety case.

steps.title: 6. PHI Scoping, Confidentiality & the Safety Case
description: Two non-negotiables sit under everything. PHI privacy: retrieval is identity-scoped to the patient and their care team (one clinician's query can't reach an unrelated patient), under HIPAA / a BAA and no-train terms, with notes and records treated as untrusted input partitioned from EHR write authority.

And a documented safety case: every recommendation traces to its sources, confidence, and safety checks, so the system is defensible — and any diagnostic claim is framed under SaMD/FDA expectations rather than shipped as an unregulated feature.

Audit is two-layered. PHI access itself is regulated and logged the moment a chart is read — who, which patient, which fields, under what care-team scope — even when the pipeline ultimately abstains and no recommendation is authorized. Recommendation audit is the separate, richer record of the evidence bundle, calibration, safety checks, and clinician decision. Conflating the two hides unauthorized access behind 'no recommendation was made'.

view.caption: PHI-scoped retrieval (only this patient, for their care team) under HIPAA/no-train; both PHI access and the recommendation itself are audited for the safety case.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep one patient's PHI from reaching another patient's query, and what makes a recommendation defensible?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: A documented, auditable argument that the system is acceptably safe: grounding, calibration, deterministic checks, human authorization, and traceable evidence.
concepts.whyItMatters: Harm-to-a-person systems must be defensible, not just functional; regulators and clinicians rely on it.
concepts.example: Each recommendation links to guideline version, chart facts used, safety-check results, and the signing clinician.

whyNow: PHI scoping and the safety case are hard requirements the gate and audit depend on.

deepDives.title: SaMD / FDA framing and dated accuracy
points: A diagnostic claim may be regulated as Software as a Medical Device — frame it accordingly, don't ship it as an unregulated feature.
Any accuracy figure is a dated claim tied to the model and population tested, not a timeless benchmark (VERIFY BEFORE PUBLISHING).
Keep raw model traces in a separate, access-controlled store under the same HIPAA/no-train terms.

recap.before: PHI and recommendations had no isolation or defensibility.
recap.after: Retrieval is PHI-scoped and every recommendation is auditable under a safety case.
recap.newRisk: Safety and calibration must be measured, not assumed.

steps.title: 7. Workflows & Safety Evaluation
description: Support the real decision-support workflows — differentials, dosing, guideline lookup, chart summarization — on this substrate, and evaluate what matters: not just accuracy, but calibration, abstention quality, and harm avoidance against clinician ground truth.
A high accuracy number that hides a few confident, harmful errors is a failure here; the eval is weighted toward catching unsafe answers and rewarding correct abstentions.

Evaluation is not a one-time launch gate but a continuous governance loop: drift detection on inputs and outputs, evaluation sliced per specialty and per task (dosing, differential, summary, guideline lookup have different risk profiles), incident review when a confident-wrong answer or an unsafe authorization slips through, controlled threshold and model-version rollout, and post-deployment surveillance as required for a clinical system. An LLM-as-judge can help triage and label trajectories, but it is an auxiliary evaluator — clinician adjudication, not the judge model, is the source of truth for clinical safety.

view.caption: Decision-support workflows run on the pipeline; clinician decisions become ground truth, and eval weights calibration, abstention quality, and harm avoidance over raw accuracy.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is raw accuracy the wrong primary metric, and what replaces it?

concepts.definition: Evaluation weighted toward catching confident-but-harmful answers and rewarding appropriate abstentions, not just average accuracy.
concepts.whyItMatters: In a harm domain the tail (a few dangerous errors) matters more than the mean.
concepts.example: Track confident-wrong rate and abstention precision/recall, not only top-line accuracy.

whyNow: Safety-weighted evaluation is what keeps the platform trustworthy as it scales.

deepDives.title: Continuous governance, not a launch gate
points: Slice evaluation per specialty and per task; a single global accuracy number averages away dangerous tails in dosing or rare presentations.
Detect drift on inputs (population, guideline changes) and outputs (rising confident-wrong or abstention rates) and trigger review before a regression reaches patients.
Gate model and threshold changes behind controlled rollout (shadow, canary, staged) with rollback, and feed every incident back into the eval set.
Keep an incident-review and post-deployment-surveillance loop; treat a confident-wrong answer that reached a clinician as a reportable event, not just a metric.

recap.after: Calibration, abstention, and harm avoidance are evaluated continuously, with drift detection, per-task slices, and incident review.
recap.newRisk: Other phase-3 verticals add real-time, contestability, and physical-experiment gates — see the plan.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Healthcare Platform
finalDesign.description: A clinical query enters through the API, which resolves care-team scope. A deterministic decision-support pipeline reasons in a bounded LLM node grounded in current guidelines and the patient's PHI-scoped record. A calibrator checks grounding sufficiency and confidence; if either is too low the sign-off & abstain gate abstains rather than guesses. A deterministic safety layer enforces contraindication, interaction, allergy, and dose rules the model cannot override. Cleared, safety-checked recommendations route to a licensed clinician for sign-off, with the authorized action bound to a clinician principal — nothing acts on the patient autonomously. PHI is scoped to the patient and care team under HIPAA/no-train, every recommendation is auditable under a documented safety case, and evaluation weights calibration, abstention quality, and harm avoidance over raw accuracy. Grounding, eval, identity/PHI scoping, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; the gate protects a person from harm, the highest stake in the series.

functional.how: Grounded reasoning on the deterministic pipeline, evaluated for safety.

functional.requirement: Ground in guidelines and the chart
functional.how: Retrieval over guidelines plus the patient record.
Calibrator routes insufficient cases to abstention.
Sign-off gate bound to a clinician principal, backed by a worklist state machine.

functional.requirement: Scope by task and risk tier
functional.how: taskType/riskTier select the calibration threshold and approval path; fail-closed on missing facts.

nonFunctional.how: Deterministic safety layer independent of the model.
Traceable sources, confidence, checks, decisions; SaMD framing.

say: Frame the gate as protecting a person from harm — the top of the irreversibility gradient, above Legal.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations; the safe default is to abstain.
Draw the agent answering directly and explain why a confident wrong answer can harm a patient.
Ground in guidelines and the chart, then the calibrator and abstain-don't-guess.
The deterministic safety layer, then clinician sign-off (recommend, never act), then PHI scoping and the safety case.
Safety-weighted eval: calibration, abstention quality, and harm avoidance, not raw accuracy.

expectations: Guideline + chart grounding and a deterministic safety layer.
Treats abstention as a first-class safe output and designs the calibrator.
Puts life-critical checks in a deterministic layer the model can't override; documents a safety case with SaMD framing.
Evaluates harm avoidance over accuracy; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you calibrate confidence well enough to decide when to abstain?
Which clinical checks belong in the deterministic safety layer vs the model?
How do you scope PHI retrieval to a patient's care team and respect minimum-necessary access?
How does the harm-to-a-person gate compare to the finance and legal gates earlier in the series?
